standard.notes: 'Un panier en osier' = a wicker basket. At French markets, shoppers traditionally bring a panier rather than using plastic bags. 'Mettre dans le panier' = to put in the basket. Also used in online shopping: 'ajouter au panier' = add to cart.
examples: J'ai un grand panier pour le marché.
I have a big basket for the market.
Elle met les légumes dans son panier.
She puts the vegetables in her basket.

standard.meaning: the checkout / the till / the cash register
standard.notes: 'Passer à la caisse' = to go to the checkout / to pay. 'La caissière' = the female cashier, 'le caissier' = the male cashier. At markets, the vendor often doubles as the cashier. Also means a crate or box: 'une caisse de tomates' = a crate of tomatoes.
examples: Je passe à la caisse — tu attends ici ?
I'll go to the checkout — will you wait here?
La caisse est au fond du magasin.
The checkout is at the back of the shop.

standard.notes: Feminine form: 'fraîche'. 'Des légumes frais' = fresh vegetables. 'Du pain frais' = fresh bread. Also means cool/chilly: 'Il fait frais ce matin' = It's cool this morning. At markets: 'C'est bien frais ?' = Is it very fresh?
examples: Ces tomates sont très fraîches.
These tomatoes are very fresh.
Il y a du poisson frais au marché.
There is fresh fish at the market.

standard.notes: Regular -er verb (with stem change: e → è before mute syllable). 'Peser les fruits' = to weigh the fruit. 'Ça pèse combien ?' = How much does it weigh? At French markets, vendors weigh produce on scales ('la balance'). 'Je pèse 60 kilos' = I weigh 60 kilos.
examples: Il pèse les pommes sur la balance.
He weighs the apples on the scales.
Je voudrais un kilo de cerises — vous pouvez les peser ?
I would like a kilo of cherries — can you weigh them?

standard.meaning: the aisle / the section / the department
standard.notes: 'Le rayon fruits et légumes' = the fruit and vegetable section. 'Le rayon boucherie' = the butcher's section. Common in supermarkets and covered markets. Also means a ray (of light): 'un rayon de soleil' = a ray of sunshine.
examples: Le rayon fromages est à droite.
The cheese section is on the right.
Où est le rayon fruits et légumes ?
Where is the fruit and vegetable section?

advanced.notes: 'Un panier en osier' = a wicker basket. At French markets, shoppers traditionally bring a panier rather than using plastic bags. 'Mettre dans le panier' = to put in the basket. Also used in online shopping: 'ajouter au panier' = add to cart.
examples: J'ai un grand panier pour le marché.
I have a big basket for the market.
Elle met les légumes dans son panier.
She puts the vegetables in her basket.

advanced.meaning: the checkout / the till / the cash register
advanced.notes: 'Passer à la caisse' = to go to the checkout / to pay. 'La caissière' = the female cashier, 'le caissier' = the male cashier. At markets, the vendor often doubles as the cashier. Also means a crate or box: 'une caisse de tomates' = a crate of tomatoes.
examples: Je passe à la caisse — tu attends ici ?
I'll go to the checkout — will you wait here?
La caisse est au fond du magasin.
The checkout is at the back of the shop.

advanced.notes: Feminine form: 'fraîche'. 'Des légumes frais' = fresh vegetables. 'Du pain frais' = fresh bread. Also means cool/chilly: 'Il fait frais ce matin' = It's cool this morning. At markets: 'C'est bien frais ?' = Is it very fresh?
examples: Ces tomates sont très fraîches.
These tomatoes are very fresh.
Il y a du poisson frais au marché.
There is fresh fish at the market.

advanced.notes: Regular -er verb (with stem change: e → è before mute syllable). 'Peser les fruits' = to weigh the fruit. 'Ça pèse combien ?' = How much does it weigh? At French markets, vendors weigh produce on scales ('la balance'). 'Je pèse 60 kilos' = I weigh 60 kilos.
examples: Il pèse les pommes sur la balance.
He weighs the apples on the scales.
Je voudrais un kilo de cerises — vous pouvez les peser ?
I would like a kilo of cherries — can you weigh them?

advanced.meaning: the aisle / the section / the department
advanced.notes: 'Le rayon fruits et légumes' = the fruit and vegetable section. 'Le rayon boucherie' = the butcher's section. Common in supermarkets and covered markets. Also means a ray (of light): 'un rayon de soleil' = a ray of sunshine.
examples: Le rayon fromages est à droite.
The cheese section is on the right.
Où est le rayon fruits et légumes ?
Where is the fruit and vegetable section?

advanced.notes: 'Vous voulez un reçu ?' = Would you like a receipt? In France, paper receipts are sometimes declined for eco reasons. 'Garder le reçu' = to keep the receipt (for returns). From the verb 'recevoir' (to receive). 'Un reçu fiscal' = a tax receipt.
examples: Vous pouvez me donner un reçu ?
Can you give me a receipt?
J'ai perdu mon reçu.
I lost my receipt.

advanced.notes: 'Faire la file' = to queue (Belgian/Swiss French). 'La file d'attente' = the waiting queue. In France: 'faire la queue' is more common. 'Il y a une longue file' = there's a long queue. Popular market stalls often have long queues.
examples: Il y a une longue file devant le stand de fromages.
There's a long queue in front of the cheese stall.
On fait la file ensemble.
We queue together.

dialogue: A
Bon, on se sépare — moi, je prends les fruits et les légumes.
B
D'accord ! Et moi, je vais au rayon fromages.
A
Tu peux peser les tomates — deux kilos, s'il te plaît.
B
Bien sûr ! Et toi, tu prends le pain aussi ?
A
Oui, moi je m'occupe du pain. On se retrouve à la caisse ?
B
Parfait. Et lui, il vient avec nous ?
A
Non, lui, il reste à la maison. On prend un grand panier ?
B
Oui — moi j'en ai un. Allons-y !

questions.question: Who takes the fruit and vegetables?
How many kilos of tomatoes does speaker A ask for?
Where do they agree to meet?

standard.summary: Two friends split up tasks at the outdoor market. Speaker A takes the fruit and vegetables (including 2 kilos of tomatoes) and the bread; speaker B goes to the cheese section. They arrange to meet at the checkout. A third person (lui) is staying at home. Speaker B has a large basket to carry everything.

dialogue: A
Moi, je connais ce marché — je viens ici tous les samedis.
B
Super ! Alors, toi, tu sais où trouver les meilleures tomates fraîches ?
A
Oui ! Le meilleur stand, c'est lui — le grand à gauche. Ses tomates sont excellentes.
B
Et eux, les autres vendeurs, ils sont bien aussi ?
A
Eux, ils sont corrects, mais lui, il est le meilleur. On prend deux kilos ?
B
D'accord. Moi, je vais peser les abricots pendant ce temps.
A
Et le fromage — toi, tu t'en occupes ? Moi, je déteste faire la file là-bas.
B
Pas de problème, moi j'y vais. On se retrouve à la caisse avec nos paniers !

questions.question: How often does speaker A come to this market?
Which stall does speaker A recommend for tomatoes?
options: The big one on the left
questions.correctAnswer: The big one on the left

questions.question: Why does speaker A not want to go to the cheese stall?
What does speaker B do while A buys tomatoes?

advanced.summary: Speaker A is a regular at this market, coming every Saturday. They recommend the big stall on the left for the best fresh tomatoes and suggest buying 2 kilos. Speaker B will weigh the apricots meanwhile. A dislikes queuing at the cheese stall, so B volunteers to handle it. They arrange to meet at the checkout with their baskets.

standard.title: Stressed / Emphatic Pronouns (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles)
standard.explanation:
Stressed pronouns (also called **emphatic** or **disjunctive** pronouns) are used to emphasise a person, especially the subject.

**The forms:**
| Subject | Stressed Pronoun |
|---|---|
| je | **moi** |
| tu | **toi** |
| il | **lui** |
| elle | **elle** |
| nous | **nous** |
| vous | **vous** |
| ils | **eux** |
| elles | **elles** |

**When to use stressed pronouns:**

**1. After prepositions (à, de, pour, avec, chez, sans…):**
- C'est **pour moi**. (It's for me.)
- Je vais **chez toi**. (I'm going to your place.)
- Il vient **avec nous**. (He comes with us.)

**2. For contrast or emphasis (often at the start of a sentence):**
- **Moi**, j'aime le marché. (I love the market.)
- **Toi**, tu prends le fromage. (You take the cheese.)
- **Lui**, il reste ici. (He stays here.)

**3. After 'c'est' / 'ce sont':**
- C'est **moi** ! (It's me!)
- Ce sont **eux**. (It's them.)

examples.french: Moi, je prends les fruits — toi, tu prends le fromage.
examples.english: I'll take the fruit — you take the cheese.

examples.french: Eux, ils attendent à la caisse.
examples.english: They are waiting at the checkout.

drills.question: Which stressed pronoun replaces 'je' for emphasis?
drills.explanation: 'Moi' is the stressed form of 'je'. Moi, je prends les fruits.

drills.question: Complete: C'est pour ___. (It's for him.)
drills.explanation: After a preposition, use the stressed pronoun. 'Pour lui' = for him.

drills.question: Which stressed pronoun replaces 'ils'?
drills.explanation: 'Eux' is the stressed form of 'ils' (masculine plural). 'Elles' is for 'elles' (feminine).

drills.question: Complete: Je vais chez ___. (I'm going to your (informal) place.)
drills.explanation: After a preposition like 'chez', use the stressed pronoun. 'Chez toi' = at your place.

drills.question: How do you say 'It's me!' in French?
drills.explanation: After 'c'est', use the stressed pronoun: C'est moi ! (never 'c'est je').

advanced.title: Stressed / Emphatic Pronouns — All Uses, Contrast & Fixed Expressions
advanced.explanation:
**Forms:**
| Subject | Stressed | Subject | Stressed |
|---|---|---|---|
| je | **moi** | nous | **nous** |
| tu | **toi** | vous | **vous** |
| il | **lui** | ils | **eux** |
| elle | **elle** | elles | **elles** |

**Uses of stressed pronouns:**

**1. After prepositions (à, de, pour, avec, chez, sans, avant, après, entre…):**
- *sans **moi*** = without me
- *entre **toi** et **moi*** = between you and me
- *avant **eux*** = before them

**2. Subject emphasis / contrast:**
- ***Moi**, je viens ici chaque semaine — **lui**, jamais.*
- ***Toi**, tu t'occupes du fromage ; **moi**, je prends les légumes.*

**3. After 'c'est' / 'ce sont':**
- *C'est **elle** qui a raison.* (She is the one who is right.)
- *Ce sont **eux** les meilleurs.* (They are the best.)

**4. Compound subjects and objects:**
- ***Toi** et **moi**, on y va ensemble.* (You and I, we go together.)
- *Il pense à **elle** et à **lui**.* (He's thinking about her and him.)

**5. Alone (one-word answers):**
- *Qui veut du fromage ? — **Moi** !* (Who wants cheese? — Me!)
- *Qui est là ? — **Eux**.* (Who's there? — Them.)

**6. With 'même' (self):**
- ***moi-même*** = myself, ***toi-même*** = yourself, ***lui-même*** = himself
- *Je l'ai fait **moi-même**.* = I did it myself.

**7. After 'ni…ni…':**
- *Ni **toi** ni **moi** ne sommes en retard.* (Neither you nor I are late.)

examples.french: Moi, je viens ici tous les samedis — lui, jamais.
examples.english: I come here every Saturday — him, never.

examples.french: C'est elle qui choisit les meilleures tomates.
examples.english: She is the one who picks the best tomatoes.

drills.question: What does 'entre toi et moi' mean?
drills.explanation: 'Entre' = between. After prepositions, use stressed pronouns: toi and moi.

drills.question: Complete: C'est ___ qui a raison. (She is the one who is right.)
drills.explanation: After 'c'est', use the stressed pronoun: c'est elle. Then 'qui' introduces the relative clause.

drills.question: How do you say 'I did it myself' using a stressed pronoun?
drills.explanation: 'Moi-même' = myself. Stressed pronoun + même for self-emphasis.

drills.question: Complete: Ni ___ ni moi ne sommes en retard. (Neither you nor I are late.)
drills.explanation: After 'ni…ni…', use stressed pronouns: ni toi ni moi.

drills.question: Qui veut du fromage ? — ___ ! (Me!)
drills.explanation: As a one-word answer, use the stressed pronoun: Moi ! (never 'Je !' alone).

drills.question: Complete: Toi et ___, on y va ensemble. (You and I, we go together.)
drills.explanation: In compound subjects 'toi et moi', both are stressed pronouns. Then use 'on' as the verb subject.

drills.question: Which is correct for 'He is the one who decides'?
drills.explanation: 'C'est + stressed pronoun + qui' = he/she is the one who…. C'est lui qui décide.

drills.question: Complete: Il pense à elle et à ___. (He's thinking about her and him.)
drills.explanation: After preposition 'à', use stressed pronoun: à elle et à lui.

standard.question: What does 'le panier' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le panier' = the basket. At French markets, shoppers traditionally bring a wicker basket.

standard.question: Which stressed pronoun replaces 'je'?
standard.explanation: 'Moi' is the stressed/emphatic form of 'je'. Used for emphasis or after prepositions.

standard.question: Complete: C'est pour ___. (It's for him.)
standard.explanation: After prepositions like 'pour', use stressed pronouns. 'Lui' = him (stressed form of il).

standard.question: What does 'frais' mean when describing food?
What does 'passer à la caisse' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Passer à la caisse' = to go to the checkout/till to pay.

advanced.question: What does 'la file d'attente' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'La file d'attente' = the waiting queue. 'Faire la queue' is the most common expression.

advanced.question: Complete: C'est ___ qui a les meilleures tomates. (She is the one with the best tomatoes.)
advanced.explanation: 'C'est + stressed pronoun + qui' = she is the one who… C'est elle qui…

advanced.question: How do you say 'I did it myself'?
What does 'le reçu' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Le reçu' = the receipt. From the verb 'recevoir' (to receive).

advanced.question: Complete: Ni toi ni ___ ne sommes en retard. (Neither you nor I are late.)
advanced.explanation: After 'ni…ni…', use stressed pronouns: ni toi ni moi.

advanced.question: What does 'peser' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Peser' = to weigh. 'Vous pouvez peser les tomates ?' = Can you weigh the tomatoes?

standard.scenario: You are at an outdoor market with a friend. Negotiate who buys what — use emphatic pronouns to say who does which task. Then order some fruit by weight from the vendor.
keyPhrases: Moi, je prends… / Toi, tu prends… (I'll take… / You take…)
Je voudrais un kilo de… (I would like a kilo of…)
Vous pouvez les peser ? (Can you weigh them?)
On se retrouve à la caisse. (We'll meet at the checkout.)
standard.modelAnswer: Moi, je prends les fruits et les légumes — toi, tu prends le fromage, d'accord ? Je vais au stand de fruits : je voudrais un kilo de tomates, s'il vous plaît. C'est frais ? Vous pouvez les peser ? Merci. On se retrouve à la caisse avec nos paniers !
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses emphatic pronouns 'moi' and 'toi' to divide tasks, orders by weight (un kilo de), asks about freshness, and arranges a meeting point at the checkout.
standard.tip: Use 'moi, je…' and 'toi, tu…' at the start of sentences to emphasise who does what — it sounds very natural in French conversation.

advanced.scenario: You are a market regular with a friend who has never been. Explain who does what using emphatic pronouns, recommend the best stalls, and handle a transaction (ordering by weight, getting a receipt).
keyPhrases: Moi, je connais ce marché. / Lui, c'est le meilleur vendeur. (I know this market. / He is the best vendor.)
C'est elle / lui qui a les meilleures… (She/He is the one with the best…)
Je l'ai fait moi-même. (I did it myself.)
Vous pouvez me donner un reçu ? (Can you give me a receipt?)
Il y a une longue file — toi, tu veux faire la queue ? (There's a long queue — do you want to queue?)
advanced.modelAnswer: Moi, je connais bien ce marché — je viens ici tous les dimanches. Toi, tu vas au rayon fromages — lui, c'est le meilleur vendeur, ses fromages sont toujours frais. Moi, je m'occupe des légumes. Je voudrais deux kilos de tomates — vous pouvez les peser ? Et un reçu, s'il vous plaît. Il y a une longue file là-bas — eux, ils attendent depuis longtemps. On se retrouve à la caisse !
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses five emphatic pronouns (moi, toi, lui, eux, moi again), recommends specific stalls using 'c'est lui qui', orders by weight, requests a receipt, and observes the queue using 'eux'.
